This work deals with the evaluation of paleoclimate conditions and related earth processes using geochemical and mineralogical proxies based on Major and Trace elements on selected External Rif Jurassic fine-grained mudrocks and marls. The used ratios will characterize the main pedogenic earth processes, particularly paleo-temperatures, paleo-precipitations, humidity and floral provinces, to endeavor a preliminary restoration of regional Jurassic paleoclimate and paleoenvironments and their setting within the regional and global paleogeographic and palaeoclimatic frameworks(Li et al., 2022; Sheldon and Tabor, 2009; Gulbranson et al., 2011; Gallagher and Sheldon, 2013; Lizzoli et al., 2021).

This study focuses on assessing the paleoclimate conditions and related earth processes by utilizing geochemical and mineralogical proxies based on major and trace elements found in selected External Rif Jurassic fine-grained mudrocks and marls. The ratios used in this analysis will characterize the primary pedogenic earth processes, specifically paleo-temperatures, paleo-precipitations, humidity, and floral provinces, to provide a preliminary reconstruction of the regional Jurassic paleoclimate and paleoenvironments and their place within the regional and global paleogeographic and palaeoclimatic frameworks (Li et al., 2022; Sheldon and Tabor, 2009; Gulbranson et al., 2011; Gallagher and Sheldon, 2013; Lizzoli et al., 2021).

This study aims to evaluate the paleoclimate conditions and related earth processes using an array of analytical tools, including geochemical and mineralogical proxies based on major and trace elements found in specifically selected External Rif Jurassic fine-grained mudrocks and marls. The ratios used in this analysis will provide insight into the key pedogenic earth processes, including paleo-temperatures, paleo-precipitations, humidity, and floral provinces. Ultimately, this study seeks to offer a preliminary restoration of the regional Jurassic paleoclimate and paleoenvironments, and to better understand their place within the broader regional and global paleogeographic and palaeoclimatic frameworks. (Li et al., 2022; Sheldon and Tabor, 2009; Gulbranson et al., 2011; Gallagher and Sheldon, 2013; Lizzoli et al., 2021).

Unraveling paleoenvironments and paleo-landscape evolution during the Jurassic of the western edge of the northwest Gondwana passive margin under paleoclimate changes during 26 Ma (Toarcian to Oxfordian) need an accurate and multidisciplinary analysis. In this foremost work, we attempt a paleogeographic and paleoclimatic reconstruction of Climate change during the early-late Jurassic. It has been generally accepted to use major and trace elements in sedimentary geology to assess paleoclimatic conditions and fluctuations during geological times (Cao et al., 2012; Armstrong-Altrin, 2020; Madhavaraju et al., 2020). Generally, elements such as Ca, Mg, K, Na, Sr, and Zn indicate arid climate, while others as Mg, Cu, Rb, Ba, Fe, Mn, V, Cr, Sr, Co, and Ni, display relatively enriched concentration in moist conditions (e.g., Ross and Bustin, 2009; Yandoka et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2016; El Mourabet et al., 2018; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Fu et al., 2018; Stankevica et al., 2020).

In order to accurately unravel the paleoenvironments and paleo-landscape evolution of the western edge of the northwest Gondwana passive margin during 26 Ma of paleoclimate changes spanning the Toarcian to Oxfordian periods, a comprehensive and interdisciplinary analysis is required. Through this primary investigation, we aim to reconstruct the paleogeographic and paleoclimatic changes that took place during the early-late Jurassic period. Major and trace elements in sedimentary geology have long been utilized as indicators of paleoclimatic conditions and fluctuations during geological times (Cao et al., 2012; Armstrong-Altrin, 2020; Madhavaraju et al., 2020). Elements such as Ca, Mg, K, Na, Sr, and Zn generally suggest arid climates, while others, such as Mg, Cu, Rb, Ba, Fe, Mn, V, Cr, Sr, Co, and Ni, display relatively enriched concentrations in moist conditions (e.g., Ross and Bustin, 2009; Yandoka et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2016; El Mourabet et al., 2018; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Fu et al., 2018; Stankevica et al., 2020).
enhance little bit please

To reveal a comprehensive understanding of the paleoenvironments and paleo-landscape evolution at the western edge of the northwest Gondwana passive margin, we must undertake an accurate and multidisciplinary analysis of paleoclimate changes spanning 26 million years, specifically during the Toarcian to Oxfordian periods. Through our research, we aim to reconstruct the paleogeographic and paleoclimatic changes that occurred during the early and late Jurassic. Major and trace elements found in sedimentary geology are commonly used to assess paleoclimate conditions and fluctuations during geological times (Cao et al., 2012; Armstrong-Altrin, 2020; Madhavaraju et al., 2020). Elements such as Ca, Mg, K, Na, Sr, and Zn are typically indicative of arid conditions, while other elements such as Mg, Cu, Rb, Ba, Fe, Mn, V, Cr, Sr, Co, and Ni are enriched in wetter environments (e.g., Ross and Bustin, 2009; Yandoka et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2016; El Mourabet et al., 2018; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Fu et al., 2018; Stankevica et al., 2020). Ultimately, our data-driven approach will seek to provide a deeper understanding of the paleoclimatic and paleoenvironmental changes that have shaped the geological history of the region.

It is noteworthy to underline that the paleoenvironmental settings may directly influence the elemental concentrations in sediments and therefore, the recorded climatic conditions such as Sr (dry–type) and Rb, Cu (wet–type) are generally used to well constrain, and to interpret the paleoclimate features (Lerman, 1978; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011 ). The Rb/Sr, Sr/Cu, and Ga/Rb elemental ratios are used as useful indicators for the paleoclimate record (Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Cao et al., 2012; Roy and Roser, 2013; Yandoka et al., 2015; Cusack et al., 2020, Roy and Roser, 2013 a; He et al., 2017, Wang et al., 2022), and Rb/K and K/Al are used as proxies for chemical weathering intensity (McLennan et al., 1993, Schneider et al., 1997; Roy et al., 2008). Strontium (Sr) and barium (Ba) can provide insights into variations of paleo-salinity. The Sr/Ba ratio is supposed to increase with increasing salinity of ambient water (Epstein and Mayeda, 1953; Meng et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2015). Lerman et al. (1995) suggested a Sr/Cu ratio threshold values between~1.3 and ~5.0 which may reflect warm and humid climates, while those > 5.0 indicate drought conditions.

It is important to highlight that paleoenvironmental settings can directly impact the elemental concentrations found in sediments, and as a result, the recorded climatic conditions. For instance, Sr (dry-type) and Rb, Cu (wet-type) are commonly utilized to accurately constrain and interpret paleoclimate features (Lerman, 1978; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011). The Rb/Sr, Sr/Cu, and Ga/Rb elemental ratios have proven to be valuable indicators of paleoclimate records (Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Cao et al., 2012; Roy and Roser, 2013; Yandoka et al., 2015; Cusack et al., 2020; Roy and Roser, 2013a; He et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2022), while Rb/K and K/Al are widely used as proxies for assessing the intensity of chemical weathering (McLennan et al., 1993; Schneider et al., 1997; Roy et al., 2008). Meanwhile, the Sr/Ba ratio is an effective tool for identifying changes in paleo-salinity, with an increase in this ratio thought to indicate an increase in the salinity of ambient water (Epstein and Mayeda, 1953; Meng et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2015). Lerman et al., (1995) proposed threshold values for the Sr/Cu ratio of around ~1.3 to ~5.0, with values above 5.0 reflecting drought conditions, and those below 1.3 indicating warm and humid climates.

Paleoenvironmental settings have a direct influence on the elemental concentrations found in sediments and can provide valuable insights into recorded climatic conditions. To accurately constrain and interpret paleoclimate features, Sr (dry-type) and Rb, Cu (wet-type) are typically used as indicators (Lerman, 1978; Liu and Zhou, 2007; Xiong and Xiao, 2011). Additionally, the Rb/Sr, Sr/Cu, and Ga/Rb elemental ratios have proven to be effective indicators for paleoclimate records (Xiong and Xiao, 2011; Cao et al., 2012; Roy and Roser, 2013; Yandoka et al., 2015; Cusack et al., 2020; Roy and Roser, 2013a; He et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2022). Assessing the intensity of chemical weathering can be achieved using Rb/K and K/Al as proxies (McLennan et al., 1993; Schneider et al., 1997; Roy et al., 2008). In addition, Sr and Ba are valuable tools for identifying changes in paleo-salinity, with an increase in the Sr/Ba ratio thought to indicate an increase in salinity levels of ambient water (Epstein and Mayeda, 1953; Meng et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2015). Lerman et al. (1995) suggested that a Sr/Cu ratio threshold value of approximately ~1.3 to ~5.0 may reflect warm and humid climates, while values above 5.0 typically indicate drought conditions.

The northern west Gondwana passive margin had undergone several climate changes during the Jurassic from late Pliensbachian p.p. (i.e., Domerian) Up to late Oxfordian. During the early Jurassic (Pliensbachian -Toarcian), the temperatures were cold. There values ranged between 9°C and 12°C with ~10.6°C on average. They decrease upward from the Pliensbachian p.p. (11.85°C) to the Toarcian (9.68°C) (Fig.5 and Table V).

The northern west Gondwana passive margin experienced multiple climate changes during the Jurassic period, beginning from the late Pliensbachian p.p. (Domerian) to the late Oxfordian. During the early Jurassic (Pliensbachian-Toarcian), temperatures were notably cold, with values ranging between 9°C to 12°C, and averaging at approximately 10.6°C. This temperature trend decreased steadily from the Pliensbachian p.p. (11.85°C) to the Toarcian (9.68°C) (see Fig. 5 and Table V for further details).

The northern west Gondwana passive margin experienced multiple climate changes during the Jurassic period, beginning from the late Pliensbachian p.p. (Domerian) to the late Oxfordian. During the early Jurassic (Pliensbachian-Toarcian), temperatures were notably cold, with values ranging between 9°C to 12°C, and averaging at approximately 10.6°C. This temperature trend decreased steadily from the Pliensbachian p.p. (11.85°C) to the Toarcian (9.68°C) (see Fig. 5 and Table V for further details).

The northern west Gondwana passive margin underwent numerous climate changes throughout the Jurassic period, beginning from the late Pliensbachian p.p. (Domerian) up to the late Oxfordian. In the early Jurassic period (Pliensbachian-Toarcian), temperatures were relatively cold, with values ranging between 9°C to 12°C, and an average temperature of approximately 10.6°C. This temperature trend steadily decreased from the Pliensbachian p.p. (11.85°C) to the Toarcian (9.68°C) (refer to Fig. 5 and Table V for further information).

The northern west Gondwana passive margin underwent a series of climate changes during the Jurassic period, stretching from the late Pliensbachian p.p. (Domerian) through to the late Oxfordian. During the early Jurassic period (Pliensbachian-Toarcian), temperatures were particularly cold, with values ranging from 9°C to 12°C, and an average temperature of around 10.6°C. This temperature trend decreased gradually from the Pliensbachian p.p. (11.85°C) to the Toarcian (9.68°C) (refer to Fig. 5 and Table V for further details).

During the Lower Jurassic, the values of the Sr/Cu ratio were high and systematically > 0.5. The Sr/Cu ratio trend decreased slightly from thePliensbachian to the Toarcian (30.36to 29.03). The Rb/Sr values were exclusively less than 0.5 indicating dry and cold conditions (e.g., Shen et al., 2006).The Rb/Sr ratio is almost below 0.5 (0.25 on average). The vertical trend shows a slight increase (0.2 to 0.28) from the Pliensbachian to Toarcian. The values of the Sr/Ba ratio are all >1 (2.24 on average), and the vertical trend of the Sr/Ba ratio decreases upward from 3.05 in the Pliensbachian to 1.64 in the Toarcian. Moreover, we record stable low values of Ga/Rb (with 0.14 on average). We note a low Mg/Ca ratio average during the early Jurassic (0.11), and the vertical trend of the Mg/Ca ratio shows an important decrease from thePliensbachian to the Toarcian (0.16-0.08) as Mg is easier to precipitate than Ca in an arid climate (Billups and Schrag, 2003; Peng et al., 2021) and therefore the Ca precipitation, may influence the Mg/Ca ratio which will decrease (Fig.5 Table IV).

During the Lower Jurassic, the Sr/Cu ratios were high and consistently above 0.5. The Sr/Cu ratio trend slightly decreased from the Pliensbachian to the Toarcian (down from 30.36 to 29.03). Rb/Sr values were consistently less than 0.5, indicating dry and cold climatic conditions (e.g., Shen et al., 2006). The Rb/Sr ratio was predominantly below 0.5, averaging at 0.25. A slight increase in the vertical trend was observed from the Pliensbachian to Toarcian (elevating from 0.2 to 0.28). Sr/Ba ratio values were recorded as all being greater than 1, averaging at 2.24. The vertical trend of the Sr/Ba ratio indicated a progressive decrease upwards, from 3.05 in the Pliensbachian to 1.64 in the Toarcian. Furthermore, stable low values of Ga/Rb (averaging at 0.14) were recorded. The early Jurassic also saw low average Mg/Ca ratio values (0.11), and the vertical trend of the Mg/Ca ratio indicated a substantial decrease from the Pliensbachian to the Toarcian (declining from 0.16-0.08).
